questions.questionText: A child presents with honey-colored crusted lesions around the nose and mouth. The most common causative organism is:
questions.optionB: Staphylococcus aureus (non-bullous impetigo — most common superficial bacterial skin infection in children; previously Strep pyogenes was predominant)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Non-bullous impetigo = honey-crusted lesions, S. aureus most common cause. Why correct: Non-bullous impetigo (70% of cases): begins as vesicles → rupture → honey-colored crusts. S. aureus is now the predominant organism (previously Group A Strep). Highly contagious. Why A wrong: Pseudomonas — ecthyma gangrenosum (in immunocompromised), hot-tub folliculitis. Why C wrong: Klebsiella — not a common skin pathogen. Why D wrong: E. coli — UTI/GI pathogen, not typical skin. Clinical relevance: Bullous impetigo = S. aureus (exfoliative toxin) — ALWAYS staphylococcal. Treatment: topical mupirocin/fusidic acid for localized; oral antibiotics (cephalexin, erythromycin) for extensive. Exam trap: Non-bullous = S. aureus OR Strep pyogenes. Bullous = ALWAYS S. aureus (exfoliative toxin A/B). Memory aid: 'Honey crusts = sweet-looking but S. aureus underneath.' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Bacterial Infections.
questions.conceptTag: Impetigo honey crust S. aureus children
